quote: I probably need more than a week, but about a week, yeah, yeah, I just… I just… I had a death in the family, that's the only reason. I know you don't have excuses for that, but, yeah, I do have a busy this week, so… by next week, I'll be freer, you know.
context: The rep asked if he was prepared to be onboarded immediately by Wednesday; he pushed back on the timeline citing a death in the family and a busy week, and the rep rescheduled onboarding for the following week.

quote: I brought everything back to my camp of people at home last night, and unfortunately, it's just not… it's not feasible right now. It was a no. It was a no.
context: The prospect returned after consulting her family and reported that they said no, opening the follow-up call with a firm decline.

quote: I just can't pile anything else on right now. It's just time… just not good at time.
context: She explained the decision was partly about being overextended, saying she already asks a lot of her family and rarely sees her husband.

quote: Yeah, I mean, everything's a burden financially when you run a business and build a new place, and… That's part of it, but it's just a lot, yeah
context: The rep probed whether finances were the real issue; she confirmed money was part of it but framed it as one of many pressures.

quote: be close, but I'll try to get it, so…
context: The rep revealed the $10,000 commercial reuse license fee and asked if it was feasible; Ray hesitated but did not refuse, and the rep noted there is flexibility on payments.

quote: That's more than I paid for my last agency, and that was a waste.
context: The rep acknowledged the past bad experience, reframed the conversation from cost to the value of a single closed deal, and backed it with a member ROI example before re-asking for the commitment.

quote: I'd really have to talk this over with my husband before I commit to anything.
context: The rep validated wanting to include the spouse, offered to join a short call with both of them so nothing got lost in relay, and set a concrete time — keeping the momentum instead of leaving it open-ended.

quote: How do I even know this actually works? I've been burned before.
context: The rep agreed the skepticism was fair, then offered to connect the prospect with two current members to hear results firsthand before deciding, turning distrust into a concrete verification step.

quote: I don't think I have the hours to actually do the work this needs.
context: The rep broke the program into the minimum weekly time and showed which parts the team handles for the member, shrinking the perceived workload; the prospect softened but wanted to confirm their schedule.
